However, some spontaneous processes can occur with a decrease in entropy, as long as the total entropy of the system and its surroundings increases. Text: Covalent bonds form between nonmetallic elements when atoms share one or more pairs of electrons to achieve a full outer energy level. The number of shared electron pairs determines the bond order, with single, double, and triple bonds forming from one, two, and three shared pairs, respectively. Text: The principles of Le Chatelier state that when a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the equilibrium will shift in a direction that tends to counteract the effect of the change. If the concentration of a reactant is increased, the equilibrium will shift to the right, consuming some of the added reactant. Conversely, if the concentration of a product is increased, the equilibrium will shift to the left. Changes in temperature and pressure also cause shifts in equilibrium. An increase in temperature will favor the endothermic direction, while a decrease in temperature will favor the exothermic direction. These principles help predict how equilibrium systems respond to changes.

Text: Electronegativity, a measure of an atom's ability to attract electrons, determines bond polarity in a covalent bond. This results in a polar covalent bond, with a dipole moment. The greater the difference in electronegativity, the more polar the bond. In extreme cases, the bond can become ionic, with electrons transferred from one atom to the other. Understanding electronegativity and bond polarity is crucial in predicting the properties of molecules, such as boiling points, solubility, and reactivity.

Bases react with water to form hydroxide ions (OH-) and conjugate acids. The strength of an acid or base depends on its ability to donate or accept protons. Strong acids and bases completely dissociate in water, while weak acids and bases only partially dissociate. Understanding acid-base reactions is essential in many areas of chemistry and biochemistry.

Text: Chromatography is a separation technique based on the differential distribution of molecules between a stationary phase and a mobile phase. As the mobile phase flows through the stationary phase, molecules separate based on their affinity for each phase. Molecules that interact strongly with the stationary phase are retained, while those that interact weakly are carried along by the mobile phase. By carefully controlling the conditions, chromatography can separate complex mixtures into individual components, which can then be identified and quantified.
